---
title: "Data Ethics Case Study 1: Big Data and Privacy"
subtitle: "Case Studies in Data Governance & Relational Systems"
author: "Brendan Shea, PhD"
date: last-modified
categories: [Big Data, Telemetry, Privacy, Smart Devices]
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    css: styles.css
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: show
    code-summary: "Show Database/SQL Reference"
    embed-resources: true
---

## Executive Summary

We live in the era of **big data**. Every digital device logs our moves automatically. Sensors stream real-time data to massive corporate files. This study looks at **data privacy** and sensory logging. We explore the balance between safety and personal freedom. We show how databases use a **staging filter** to clean raw records. This design helps protect privacy while keeping valuable trends.

::: {.callout-note}
### Core Concepts Covered
*   We study how to manage the massive flow of sensory records using staging files.
*   We explore the three core features of modern databases: volume, velocity, and variety.
*   We learn how to filter out sensitive details before saving data permanently.
:::

---

## The Scenario

### Telemetry on the Starship Enterprise

In the classic television show *Star Trek*, the Starship Enterprise travels the deep galaxy. The ship is a marvel of advanced technology. It constantly scans alien planets and cosmic storms. Behind the scenes, Starfleet Command monitors the ship from Earth. 

Computers log every shield-up event. Sensors record warp speed changes. Bio-sensors stream the heartbeats of the crew in real time. The database logs engine heat levels, phaser battery levels, and transporter beam frequencies. It even logs the cabin temperatures of every room on the ship.

This massive flow of sensory records is called **telemetry data**. Telemetry means automated readings sent from a distance. For Starfleet, this data is vital for survival. It helps engineers detect hull cracks before they break. It helps doctors spot sick crew members instantly. 

But it also represents the classic features of **big data**. We can understand big data through three core features:

1.  First, we look at **volume**. Volume means the sheer physical size of the data. The Enterprise logs millions of sensor readings every second. A single ship generates gigabytes of logs per day. Across a fleet of five hundred starships, Starfleet must store petabytes of data. Traditional paper logs are completely useless at this scale. Starfleet must rely on massive relational databases to organize these records.

2.  Second, we look at **velocity**. Velocity means the extreme speed of the data. Sensor readings must stream back to Earth instantly. When the ship enters a dangerous space storm, sensors detect hull pressure changes in microseconds. This data must travel across lightyears via subspace signals, update the central database, and trigger warning lights on the bridge console immediately. Any delay in velocity could destroy the ship.

3.  Third, we look at **variety**. Variety means the different formats of the data. The database cannot just store simple numbers. It must store shield status numbers, engine temperature charts, and crew medical text reports. It must also log raw video files from external cameras and complex 3D coordinate grids from navigation sensors. The database engine must organize all these different file types into a single, cohesive archive.

This data helps Starfleet keep the crew safe. But it also records every single crew member's private life. It logs where they walk, how they feel, and what they do. It tracks when they sleep and who they talk to.

### The Economics of Data Storage

This explosion of sensory logging is driven by the economics of technology. In the 1980s, database storage was extremely expensive. Storing a single gigabyte of data could cost over ten thousand dollars. Because of this high cost, database designers were very careful. They only saved the most important financial transactions. They deleted temporary logs immediately to save disk space.

But over the last forty years, the cost of database storage has dropped to almost zero. Today, storing a gigabyte of data in the cloud costs less than a penny. Because storage is so cheap, corporate database engineers no longer worry about deleting records. 

They build systems to harvest everything. They log every click, every location coordinate, and every sensor reading. They store this raw data indefinitely in massive data warehouses, hoping to analyze it for commercial profit later.

### From Starfleet to Smart Homes

Today, we do not need a starship to experience this level of logging. Modern homes are filled with connected sensors. Smart thermostats track our daily schedules. Smart watches count our steps and heart rates. Security cameras log our movements.

These devices stream details back to corporate databases. This creates a massive record of our personal lives. The companies say this data makes our lives easier. But it also creates a permanent digital footprint. It allows tech companies to build a deep, personal profile of our home habits.

### The Real-World battles

This continuous harvesting has led to major real-world clashes:

First, consider the **Strava heatmap leak**. In 2018, a fitness app company named Strava released a public map called the global heatmap. The incident was widely covered, including in major investigative reports by [The Guardian](https://www.theguardian.com/world/2018/jan/28/fitness-tracking-app-strava-gives-away-location-of-secret-us-army-bases), which detailed how the app exposed the locations of secret military bases. The map showed every run, walk, and bike ride recorded by its millions of users worldwide. The map looked beautiful, showing bright glowing lines across dark landmasses. 

But experts soon noticed a massive problem. In remote desert war zones, the map showed bright glowing paths in the middle of empty sand. These paths were recorded by soldiers jogging around secret military bases. The soldiers wore connected fitness watches. 

The watches tracked their exact GPS coordinates and uploaded the data to Strava's databases. The database had accidentally mapped the exact layouts of top-secret bases in war zones like Syria and Afghanistan. It showed where the barracks were, where patrols walked, and where the command centers sat. A database built to share workouts had leaked national security secrets.

Journalists at *The Daily Beast* and military analysts used this public database to track patrols outside active bases in Helmand Province, Afghanistan. They proved that even in the middle of a war zone, the digital footprint of a fitness app could expose critical operational secrets.

Second, consider the **smart thermostat tracking** debate. Millions of smart thermostats stream home temperature logs to corporate databases. These devices contain motion sensors and light detectors. 

They do not just control the heat. They build a database of when families are home, when they sleep, and when they travel. Tech companies can sell this data to utility companies, insurance firms, or advertising networks. This reveals private home patterns without clear customer choice. It turns our private home habits into a commercial product.

---

## The Ethical Dilemma

This massive growth of sensory data raises two major ethical conflicts.

### Safety Surveillance versus Personal Freedom

The first conflict is about safety and surveillance. This debate fits the theory of **surveillance capitalism** (coined by writer Shoshana Zuboff). Surveillance capitalism is a business model where companies harvest private human experiences to sell targeted ads. 

Organizations argue that they must log data to protect people or manage resources. But this creates a total surveillance state where private spaces disappear.

The argument for telemetry logging is structured as follows:

::: {.argument-block}
::: {.argument-premise}
**Premise 1**: Keeping soldiers safe, protecting national security, and tracking equipment health is necessary for public safety.
:::
::: {.argument-premise}
**Premise 2**: Collecting real-time sensory data is the only way to detect equipment failures and spot safety threats instantly.
:::
::: {.argument-conclusion}
**Conclusion**: Therefore, organizations have a moral duty to log sensory telemetry from all connected devices.
:::
:::

Advocates argue that the benefits of big data are too large to ignore. In healthcare, tracking vitals can predict a heart attack before it happens. In city planning, tracking traffic speeds can prevent gridlock. For these supporters, privacy is a small price to pay for safety and efficiency.

The argument against telemetry logging is structured as follows:

::: {.argument-block}
::: {.argument-premise}
**Premise 1**: Individuals have a basic right to privacy, which includes their physical movements and home habits.
:::
::: {.argument-premise}
**Premise 2**: Collecting permanent sensory logs creates an inescapable tracking system that can be leaked or abused.
:::
::: {.argument-conclusion}
**Conclusion**: Therefore, logging detailed personal sensory telemetry without active boundaries violates basic rights.
:::
:::

Opponents argue that constant logging alters human behavior. When people know they are being watched, they act differently. They become less creative and more fearful. This tracking harms personal freedom and cognitive choice.

### The Dilemma of Metadata

The second dilemma is about **metadata** (data about data). Companies often argue that they protect privacy by removing names from their databases. They claim that anonymous GPS coordinates are completely safe. 

But the Strava leak proved this claim is completely false. Even without names, a database of coordinates reveals private realities. If a runner jogs the same loop every morning, it is easy to find where they live. Removing a name does not make data anonymous. 

### The Mechanics of Deanonymization

When databases combine location, time, and frequency, **deanonymization** is very simple. We can cross-reference the anonymous logs with public home records to find the exact name of the user. True anonymity is almost impossible in a big data world.

Consider this step-by-step walkthrough of how a database administrator can easily deanonymize "anonymous" coordinates:

1.  First, the administrator queries the fitness database for all running logs that start and end at the exact same geographic coordinate between 6:00 AM and 7:00 AM.

2.  Second, the administrator exports these coordinates and plugs them into a public land mapping database, such as a county land deeds website.

3.  Third, the land registry returns the physical address of the home matching those coordinates, along with the names of the property owners.

By linking these two databases, the "anonymous" fitness logs are instantly connected to a real, living person's name and home address. This process can be executed in seconds, proving that metadata is a major privacy risk.

---

## Database & Data Governance Considerations

To protect privacy, modern organizations use strong **data governance** (data rules). They do not save raw sensory feeds directly. Instead, they design database systems to act as a **staging filter**. 

A staging filter is a temporary database zone. It isolates and cleans raw data before sending it to a permanent archive.

### Graphic Depiction of the Schema

The following diagram outlines an ethical staging database. It shows how raw telemetry logs are filtered, sanitized, and stored:

```{mermaid}
erDiagram
    RAW_TELEMETRY ||--o{ STAGING_FILTERS : "feeds into"
    STAGING_FILTERS ||--o{ APPROVED_LOGS : "clears into"
    
    RAW_TELEMETRY {
        string log_id
        string device_id
        string gps_coordinates
        string heart_rate_reading
    }
    
    STAGING_FILTERS {
        string filter_id
        string raw_log_id
        string anonymize_status
        string geographic_mask_applied
    }
    
    APPROVED_LOGS {
        string archive_id
        string masked_device_id
        string generalized_region
        string average_heart_rate
    }
```

### Detailed Attribute Descriptions

To understand how this data history system works, let us look at the key details stored in our database tables:

First, consider the `RAW_TELEMETRY` table. This table is a temporary landing zone. It stores the `log_id`, which is a unique number for each incoming reading. It logs the `device_id` (the device serial number), the raw `gps_coordinates` (exact latitude and longitude), and the `heart_rate_reading`. This table holds highly sensitive, raw personal details.

Second, consider the `STAGING_FILTERS` table. This table manages the cleaning process. The `filter_id` tracks each active filter job. The `raw_log_id` links the job back to the temporary landing table. The `anonymize_status` tracks whether the cleaning is finished. The `geographic_mask_applied` records whether coordinates have been generalized.

Third, consider the `APPROVED_LOGS` table. This is our permanent archive. It stores the `archive_id` for long-term records. The `masked_device_id` replaces the unique device serial number with a random code. The `generalized_region` replaces exact coordinates with a broad zip code. The `average_heart_rate` replaces raw, second-by-second medical feeds with a broad daily average. This table contains zero personal details, making it safe for long-term storage and research.

### Structured Logic for Compliance Filtering

Instead of writing complex code, database designers enforce ethics directly in the database structure. The database acts as a digital gatekeeper. It checks every scraped file before it is used for training. 

The database performs a three-step cleaning process:

1.  First, when a device streams new telemetry, the system saves it in a temporary raw table. This table is kept separate from the main archive.

2.  Second, the database runs a cleaning program. It strips away precise GPS coordinates. It masks unique device serial numbers.

3.  Third, the system evaluates the data against compliance rules to ensure it is safe to keep.

We can write this filter as a set of simple, logical database rules:

*   First, our **permissions check** blocks any log if the user has disabled location sharing in their account.

*   Second, our **geographic mask** generalizes coordinates, converting exact street addresses into broad zip codes.

*   Third, our **data retention rule** deletes all raw logs from the temporary table after 24 hours.

In practice, the system combines these rules into a single check:

*   **IF** location sharing is active **AND** coordinates are generalized **AND** device identifiers are masked, **THEN** save the cleaned record into the approved logs archive.

*   **IF** any of these requirements are violated, **THEN** immediately reject the log and delete the raw record.

By using this temporary staging area, organizations can study broad fitness or temperature trends. But they can never be used to track a specific person's home schedule or secret movements.

### Enforcing Purges and Erasure Logs

When a creator sends a copyright removal request or invokes their privacy rights, the data system must execute a two-step purge process to maintain legal and ethical integrity:

1.  The system records the document's web address, the reason for deletion (such as a GDPR erasure request), and the exact timestamp in a secure audit log table.

2.  The system deletes the original document from the database and flags the parent domain to ensure that future automated web crawlers can never harvest files from that website again.

By maintaining a permanent audit trail of all deleted files, the organization can prove to regulators and creators that it respects digital boundaries.

---

## Discussion Questions

1.  In the Star Trek scenario, Starfleet Command logs biosensor data to protect the crew during danger. Do you think a military branch has the right to monitor soldiers' health and location when they are off duty? Where should we draw the line between safety and privacy?

2.  Create a real-world analogy for fitness tracking heatmaps. Is it like a runner leaving physical footprints on a muddy public trail? Or is it like a tracker following them with a video camera and posting the tape online? Explain the strengths and weaknesses of your analogy.

3.  Our database diagram generalizes location details to protect users. But what if a city planning office needs precise GPS logs to fix dangerous roads? How can a database manager balance the need for precise details with the need for privacy?

4.  Fitness trackers gather location data. If a user commits a crime, should the police be allowed to search the company's database to find where the suspect was? How might this impact public trust in smart devices?

5.  Imagine you are the data manager at a fitness app company. Write a simple three-sentence privacy policy for your users. Address how you will handle raw GPS logs, public map sharing, and user requests to delete their tracks.

---

## References

*   **Strava Heatmap Leak Coverage**: Hern, Alex. "Fitness tracking app Strava gives away location of secret US army bases." *The Guardian*, 28 Jan. 2018, [theguardian.com/world/2018/jan/28/fitness-tracking-app-strava-gives-away-location-of-secret-us-army-bases](https://www.theguardian.com/world/2018/jan/28/fitness-tracking-app-strava-gives-away-location-of-secret-us-army-bases). This investigative report details how aggregated fitness data accidentally mapped top-secret military outposts in active conflict zones.

---

## Key Takeaways

*   Every database collecting sensory data must use a staging filter to isolate and sanitize raw records before permanent storage.

*   Removing names does not guarantee privacy. Location, time, and frequency coordinates can be combined to identify specific users.

*   Active compliance rules must be built into the database layer to automatically delete temporary raw telemetry records after a short period.
